title: Waiting for the Lord
speaker: Pastor Tito Lyro
source_files: 05-21-23 - Waiting for the Lord - Psalm 130 - Pastor Tito Lyro.txt

outline.title: The Depths and the Cry to God
subpoints: The Psalmist begins from the depths — the miry pit
Sin talk and forgiveness talk provide the stairway out
The Psalmist knows God as a forgiving God and that grounds his waiting

outline.title: Waiting and Hoping in God's Word
subpoints: Wait and hope are synonyms in this context — to wait for is to hope in
Calvin: when a man embraces the word, he becomes assured of his welfare; assurance is the mother of waiting
The soul-deep hope compared to the watchman waiting for the morning
The watchman knows the sun is coming even before it rises — he waits with confidence

outline.title: Testimony to Others: God's Inexhaustible Mercy
subpoints: Psalm 130:7-8: O Israel, hope in the Lord — the Psalmist extends his testimony outward
With the Lord there is mercy and abundant redemption
God's mercy and forgiveness cannot be exhausted
The miry pit to the rock: Psalm 40 parallel — God puts a new song in the mouth

outline.title: Four Questions of Application
subpoints: 1. Do you believe that seeing sin in yourself is a good thing?
2. Do you believe that sin is against God?
3. Do you believe that sin is found in motive, thoughts, and deeds — not just actions?
4. Can you pinpoint right now a handful of sins that you see in your life?

summary:
Pastor Tito Lyro preaches from Psalm 130, a psalm of ascent that moves from the depths of despair and conviction of sin to confident hope in God's forgiving character. He notes that Psalm 130 is often brought into conversations about depression, and while he is careful to acknowledge its complexity, he highlights that it is fundamentally sin talk and forgiveness talk that provide the Psalmist with the stairway out of the depths. Because the Psalmist knows God as a forgiving God, he is able to wait.

The sermon traces the Psalm's arc from cry to waiting to testimony. Waiting and hoping are treated as synonyms: to wait for God is to hope in God. John Calvin's meditation on the psalm is cited: when a man embraces the Word, he becomes assured of God's attention to his welfare, and that assurance becomes the mother of both waiting and patience. The Psalmist illustrates his hope with the image of a watchman on the city wall during the last, darkest, coldest watch of the night — eyes fixed on the horizon for the rising sun, knowing it is coming. The sun is identified through Malachi 4:2 as the Son of Righteousness, Jesus Christ himself.

The sermon closes with four diagnostic questions for self-examination: whether believers see sin-awareness as a good thing (as evidence of the Spirit's nearness), whether they understand sin as being against God personally, whether they recognize sin in motive and thought and not merely in action, and whether they can identify specific current sins in their lives. The inability to name any specific sin is presented as a serious spiritual warning sign — the beauty and relief of God's forgiveness can only be appreciated when one knows concretely what is being forgiven.
transcript:
## The Depths and the Cry to God

That we want to bring into any conversation around the topic of depression yet. It is sin talk. It is forgiveness talk that the Psalmist finds great hope in. I hope that provides steps or a stairway for him to get out of the depths he finds himself in. Because he knows God as a forgiving God, he is able to wait for him.

## Waiting and Hoping

In verses 5 and 6, he says, "I wait for the Lord, my soul waits and in his word I do hope. My soul waits for the Lord more than those who watch for the morning." He waits for the Lord. And wait and hope mean the same thing in this context. To wait for is to hope in.

John Calvin in meditating on this Psalm says, when a man embraces the word, it becomes assured of having his welfare attended to by God. This assurance will be the mother of waiting and of patience.

In the verse, this is not an outward-only sort of hope, but a soul-deep hope. The Psalm illustrates his hope for the Lord with the watchman who is keeping guard on the wall in verse 6. The watchman during the last watch of the night, who fixes his eyes on the horizon in great expectation for the sun to come up. He didn't wonder if the sun was coming up. He knew the sun was coming up, but he couldn't wait for the sun to come up. He knew it was coming up, and with it, the shadows where the enemies could hide would be dissipated.

So he watched the horizon waiting for the sun to come up. When I first got married, I worked for UPS — United Parcel Service. And we were in a shift called the preload, which would be early in the morning, and we'd go in and load up the little brown trucks. Because the building that we worked in was too small to have the trucks parked and the doors closed, in essence it was a building without walls because all the bay doors would be up. And in winter times it would be super, super cold. And right before dawn — dawn is the morning, right? Dusk is the other — right before dawn was the coldest hour of the shift. And we would be just waiting for the sun to break the horizon because then the temperature would increase by quite a few degrees and our fingers would defrost for a few minutes.

That's the picture. Just waiting for the sun to come in the morning. In the repetition that he gives us here, it gives the impression that this wait can be extended and painful, but also that the Psalmist is convinced the sun is coming. And to bring the words of a separate song, Psalm 30, verse 5 into this one: the Watchman was excited about the morning because "joy comes with the rising of the sun, even if after a difficult night." Because the Sun — with a capital S — is the Lord Jesus Christ himself. That's what the prophet Malachi told us. In Malachi 4:2, he says, "to you who fear my name, the Son of Righteousness shall arise with healing in his wings and you shall go out and grow fat like stall-fed calves."

## Testimony to Others

So the Psalmist was starting from the bottom of the pit. He cried out to the Lord and his hope was in God. He got a rising stairway up out of the miry pit, and then he ends the song by testifying about the Lord. Look at verses 7 and 8: "O Israel, hope in the Lord, for with the Lord there is mercy. And with him is abundant redemption and he shall redeem Israel from all his iniquities."

So the extension from the depths is completed when the Psalmist declares to others the work of God in Christ — the work that God has done in Christ in him. And the Psalmist is experiencing here a new song that God has placed in his heart. He reflects Psalm 40, verses 1 through 3, where the Psalmist says, "I was in a miry pit in darkness" — the idea of being in a well dug up, in the bottom, set of water, just miry mud, and you're stuck, and the more you move the more stuck you get, and when you try to get out on your own, the more you get stuck. And you look up and you can just see a little bit of light at the mouth of the well.

And then the Psalmist says that when the Lord took me out, he put me upon a rock — stability, no sinking, stability. And that rock is Jesus Christ. And he gave me a new song. That's what Psalm 130 is experiencing right there — deliverance from the Lord. And he wants others to know and have the same hope as he does.

And this testimony of the Psalmist is that God's mercy and forgiveness cannot be exhausted. Because God's redemption is abundant, and in his lovingkindness, the sinner doesn't have to despair. And this is the message for the church of Jesus Christ: "O Israel, hope in the Lord." So church, where is your hope? What are you hoping in? What is it that you're holding on to in this life? God redeems his church from all her iniquities. We see that in verse 8.

## Four Questions of Application

Let me end here with four questions of application.

First: do you believe that seeing sin in yourself is a good thing? Here you are, feeling like your self-worth is going to go lower, and I ask you to acknowledge that you are a saint who still sins, who currently has sinful thoughts, words, and deeds in your life. But brothers and sisters, contrary to popular belief, it is a good thing when you see sin in yourself, for at least two reasons. Sin might feel natural to us today, but we were originally created to live without it. God's design for us is a life without sin. We don't experience that sinlessness on this side of heaven, but when we battle sin, we get a taste of how we were intended to live. And when we are forgiven by God, like the Psalmist, we find joy and hope because we are restored to how we are supposed to exist.

The second reason why this is important: when we see sin in us, it is evidence that God is close to us. Everyone thought about that? When we actually recognize sin in our hearts and are convicted of it, it is actually evidence that God is close to us. It is the Holy Spirit revealing sin. Our Lord Jesus Christ said, "When the Holy Spirit has come, he will convict the world of sin and righteousness and of judgment." So if you see sin in your life, have hope because the Holy Spirit is at work in you. Knowledge of sin is tangible evidence of God's love for you.

Second question: do you believe that sin is against God? We don't immediately recognize that sin is conscious rebellion against God — only when the Holy Spirit works in our hearts can we see that. But when we understand that sin is against God, we have great hope because God is a forgiving God.

Question number three: do you believe that sin is found in motive, thoughts, and deeds? Not just in our actions. We may be able to go through a day with others not actually seeing our sin. But at the level of the human heart, you will find selfishness and pride and sins of that nature. When we are able to see that our sins go beyond our actions and are rooted in our hearts, then we have hope, because God's own family love in Jesus Christ changes hearts. Not just actions but hearts.

And lastly: can you pinpoint right now a handful of sins that you see in your life? Take a second right now — can you think of a handful of sins that you see in your life? Don't think, "Oh, I can be a better husband." That's too general. Can you think of specific sins that you're presently sinning against God? Things that are present in your life? Start with the more obvious ones and keep going. If you have trouble finding them, read through the Ten Commandments. And if you still have trouble, then you may not know Jesus. If you really cannot see sin in your life, it is an evidence that you're not believing in Jesus Christ. If you fail in this, then this promise — namely, God's forgiveness — is for you. Because you really want to appreciate the forgiveness of God when you recognize that you sin. Otherwise there's no beauty in that.

The Psalmist knew that his deepest problem was sin. He also knew that his God, the triune God of the Scriptures, did not keep a record of wrongs for all those who turned to him. The Psalmist anticipated the cross of Christ where God placed the just penalty for the rebellion of his creatures on his Son. "For scarcely for a righteous man will one die, yet perhaps for a good man someone would even dare to die. But God demonstrates his own love toward us in that while we are still sinners, Christ died for us."

Let us pray together. Father, have mercy. Thank you for your word. We pray that your Spirit would use it for the reviving of your church, for the saving of the elect, and for the purifying of the believer. We pray this in Jesus' name. Amen.
